channel.title: NYT > U.S. News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Charges Against Man Who Drove Family Off Cliff Are Dropped After Treatment
item.description: Three counts of attempted murder against Dharmesh A. Patel were dismissed after he completed a court-ordered mental health program, prosecutors said.
item.dc:creator: Mack Liederman

item.title: Haley Stevens Goes on Attack Against Abdul El-Sayed at Michigan Senate Debate
item.description: At their first one-on-one debate in a top Senate race, Representative Haley Stevens accused her progressive rival, Dr. Abdul El-Sayed, of being too focused on publicity as he called her a tool of corporate interests.
item.dc:creator: Reid J. Epstein
item.media:credit: Bryan Esler/WOOD TV8
item.media:description: Dr. Abdul El-Sayed and Representative Haley Stevens met on Tuesday night in Grand Rapids for their first Democratic primary debate since a third candidate dropped out.

item.title: Dozens Face Felony Charges After Raid of Beagle Research Facility
item.description: The charges for most of the people carry sentences of up to 12.5 years in prison, officials said, and the police have recommended more charges for a separate attempt in April.
item.dc:creator: Madaleine Rubin
item.media:credit: Taylor Glascock for The New York Times
item.media:description: Activists were enveloped in tear gas during an attempt to raid Ridglan Farms to rescue beagles in Blue Mounds, Wis., in April.

item.title: Top Maine Democrat Says Platner Will Have ‘No Role’ in Picking Next Nominee
item.description: A top state party official said that Graham Platner’s team had “repeatedly” tried to “put their thumb on the scale,” but he would not influence how the party chose a new Senate candidate.
item.dc:creator: Shane Goldmacher
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Graham Platner has faced widespread calls from top Democrats to end his Senate campaign after a woman accused him of rape.

item.title: Judge Orders D.H.S. to Restore 4 States’ Access to Citizenship Data
item.description: The ruling, based on an agreement the Trump administration signed with Florida last year, contradicted an earlier order by a judge in Washington that required the access be suspended.
item.dc:creator: Zach Montague and David Ovalle
item.media:credit: Zack Wittman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Election workers counting ballots in Largo, Fla., in 2024. An order by a federal judge in Florida on Tuesday allowed bulk searches of a federal citizenship database by state officials to check voter eligibility.

item.title: Part of Florida’s ‘Stop WOKE’ Law Ruled Illegal by Appeals Court
item.description: The law limits teaching on race, gender and other topics. Part of the law related to colleges was struck down. It remains in effect in K-12 schools.
item.dc:creator: Vimal Patel
item.media:credit: Chris O'Meara/Associated Press
item.media:description: Gov. Ron DeSantis of Florida signed the “Stop WOKE” Act to limit teaching about race, gender and other topics.

item.title: As Israel Loses Support in the U.S., Rahm Emanuel Criticizes Netanyahu
item.description: The former Chicago mayor, a Democrat exploring a 2028 presidential run, is in Tel Aviv calling for an end to unconditional U.S. support of Israel.
item.dc:creator: David M. Halbfinger
item.media:credit: Jason Andrew for The New York Times
item.media:description: Rahm Emanuel, the former Chicago mayor and White House chief of staff, is trying to chart a course between the anti-Israel left and the pro-Israel right.

item.title: U.S. Strikes Iran and Reimposes Sanctions After Tanker Attacks
item.description: The military operation came hours after the U.S. Treasury revoked a waiver allowing global sales of Iranian oil.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: Negotiations between Iran and the United States have been paused until after multiday funeral ceremonies in Tehran for Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, the supreme leader who was killed on the first day of the war.

item.title: These Maine Democrats Might Replace Graham Platner in the Senate Race
item.description: Mr. Platner has not yet withdrawn from the race after a rape allegation, but Democrats in the state and nationally are already exploring potential alternatives.
item.dc:creator: Bayliss Wagner
item.media:credit: Cj Gunther/Getty Images
item.media:description: A wide range of Democrats, including many former allies, have called on Graham Platner to end his campaign for Senate in Maine.

item.title: Maryland Moves Toward Redistricting Ahead of 2028
item.description: Lawmakers at a special legislative session next month will try to pass an amendment to the state constitution that should ensure new congressional maps survive a legal challenge.
item.dc:creator: Campbell Robertson
item.media:credit: Robert Yoon/Associated Press
item.media:description: The Maryland State House in Annapolis.

item.title: Judge Quashes Justice Dept. Subpoena in 2020 Election Inquiry
item.description: The ruling cast serious doubts on the department’s efforts to reinvestigate the race, finding that it was too late to bring criminal charges in the case.
item.dc:creator: Alan Feuer
item.media:credit: Nicole Craine for The New York Times
item.media:description: Members of the F.B.I. outside of the Fulton County elections office in Georgia this year.

item.title: Utah Moves to Close Teen Treatment Center Accused of Abusing Girls
item.description: Paris Hilton helped lead a campaign to shut down the Provo Canyon School, where she said she was beaten and spat on when she was 17.
item.dc:creator: Michael Levenson
item.media:credit: Hannah Schoenbaum/Associated Press
item.media:description: Paris Hilton, the celebrity socialite, outside a Provo Canyon School building in Springville, Utah, last month. She has described being beaten at the school when she was a teenager.

item.title: ICE Officer Kills a Mexican Man in Houston
item.description: The agency said the man was an “illegal alien” who had tried to run over the agent, though no evidence was immediately offered.
item.dc:creator: Edgar Sandoval
item.media:credit: Jacob Lujan/Houston Chronicle, via Associated Press
item.media:description: The Houston Fire Department said the man was shot in the abdomen.

item.title: Justice Department Threatens Top Election Officials Over Noncitizen Voting
item.description: The letters come amid President Trump’s effort to tighten election rules to prevent a problem that doesn’t exist: widespread noncitizen voting.
item.dc:creator: Nick Corasaniti
item.media:credit: Andrew Harnik/Getty Images
item.media:description: Letters from Harmeet Dhillon, who runs the Justice Department’s civil rights division, to all 50 states and the District of Columbia threatened prosecution of top election officials if votes by noncitizens were counted in upcoming elections.

item.title: Is Haiti Safe for Haitian Deportees?
item.description: Stephen Miller said that Haiti is safe for Haitian deportees. Our White House correspondent Zolan Kanno-Youngs analyzed federal reports that say otherwise.
item.dc:creator: Zolan Kanno-Youngs, Gilad Thaler, Jon Miller, Ray Whitehouse and Stephanie Swart

item.title: U.S. Revokes Iran Oil Sanctions Waiver After Attack on Tankers
item.description: A 60-day exemption allowing the sale of Iranian oil was cut short after three tankers were attacked in the Strait of Hormuz.
item.dc:creator: Alan Rappeport
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Reuters
item.media:description: A vessel last week in the Strait of Hormuz near Musandam, Oman. Iran has asserted that it has sole control of the waterway.

item.title: Andrea Shaw, Plaintiff in Anti-Vaccine Suit, Is Charged With Murder of Her Twins
item.description: A lawsuit filed by Children’s Health Defense, an organization founded by Robert F. Kennedy Jr., claims that vaccines caused the twins’ deaths. But a grand jury in Idaho has indicted her on charges of suffocating them.
item.dc:creator: Christina Jewett
item.media:credit: Bonnie Cash/Reuters
item.media:description: Children’s Health Defense’s chief executive said the group planned to stand by Andrea Renee Shaw’s claim that vaccines caused the toddlers’ deaths.

item.title: Authorities Arrest Members of India-Based Gangs in Sweeping Crackdown
item.description: The actions were announced in Los Angeles, and most of the new arrests were in California. One of the gangs is accused of assassinating a prominent Sikh separatist leader in British Columbia.
item.dc:creator: Shawn Hubler
item.media:credit: Darryl Dyck/The Canadian Press, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Hardeep Singh Nijjar, a Sikh nationalist leader from India’s Punjab state, was shot and killed in June 2023 at a temple he led in Surrey, British Columbia. The authorities said that an India-based gang was responsible for the killing.

item.title: Platner Supporters Struggle to Come to Terms With Campaign’s Death Knell
item.description: Many voters said they expected Graham Platner, the state’s Democratic nominee for Senate, to exit the race after a former girlfriend publicly accused him of sexual assault.
item.dc:creator: Jenna Russell
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: The high-profile race, considered critical to Democrats’ efforts to take control of the Senate, has been roiled by a series of concerning revelations about Graham Platner, the party’s nominee.

item.title: Republican Leaders Say They’ve Spoken With Mitch McConnell Since Hospitalization
item.description: Two top Republican senators said they had talked to Senator Mitch McConnell, who has been hospitalized since June 14, but did not offer updates on his status or a timeline for his return.
item.dc:creator: Catie Edmondson
item.media:credit: Al Drago for The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Mitch McConnell of Kentucky arriving for a vote at the Capitol this year. Mr. McConnell has been hospitalized since June 14.

item.title: Bernie Sanders Calls on Graham Platner to Drop Senate Bid After Rape Allegation
item.description: Mr. Sanders was one of the last remaining prominent allies of Graham Platner, the Maine Senate Democratic nominee, to call for him to drop out after a rape allegation.
item.dc:creator: Bayliss Wagner
item.media:credit: Heather Khalifa for The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Bernie Sanders of Vermont had defended Graham Platner after previous scandals, but called on him on Tuesday to drop out of the Maine Senate race.

item.title: Yale Law School Fights to Stop Trump Deal
item.description: The university has been in talks to resolve investigations into its admissions practices. The law school’s dean and some faculty have argued a deal could compromise its independence.
item.dc:creator: Alan Blinder and Michael S. Schmidt
item.media:credit: Christopher Capozziello for The New York Times
item.media:description: Students at Yale Law School have also criticized the university’s efforts to seek a deal with the Trump administration.

item.title: Lawsuit Alleges Trump Administration Gave Iran Details on Asylum Seekers
item.description: A new lawsuit filed in the District of Columbia states that the administration allowed Iranian officials to “select” which Iranians seeking refuge in the United States would be expelled.
item.dc:creator: Zach Montague
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: A billboard showing Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, the slain Iranian leader, at an intersection in Tehran in May.

item.title: The Accused Is in Court but Conspiracy Theories Still Swirl Around Kirk Case
item.description: Mr. Kirk’s assassination played out live on the internet, but since then, it has become fodder for fantasy.
item.dc:creator: Robert Draper
item.media:credit: Kim Raff for The New York Times
item.media:description: Keli Rabon, a podcaster from Houston, filming outside the courthouse in Provo, Utah, on the first day of a pre-trial hearing for Tyler Robinson, who was charged with killing Charlie Kirk last year.

item.title: The World Cup Stood as a Break From U.S. Politics. Then Trump Got Involved.
item.description: The president’s call to the head of FIFA underscores that he has yet to find an area of American, or even global, life where he is unwilling to assert himself.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump accepting the newly created “FIFA Peace Prize” from Gianni Infantino, the FIFA president, last year. Mr. Trump called Mr. Infantino to protest an American player’s suspension.

item.title: Democrats Clash Over Who Replaces Platner Even Before He Exits
item.description: Progressives and moderates are gearing up for a fight over an as-yet-undecided process in Maine to name a Senate nominee to replace Graham Platner after a rape accusation. He still wants a say, too.
item.dc:creator: Shane Goldmacher, Reid J. Epstein and Katie Glueck
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: More Democratic allies of Graham Platner called on Tuesday for him to end his campaign for Senate after a woman accused him of rape in a report on Monday.

item.title: Suspect Interacted With Charlie Kirk’s Group Before Killing, Prosecutors Say
item.description: In a hearing, prosecutors used videos to trace the steps of the man they accuse of assassinating Mr. Kirk, while defense lawyers tried to poke holes in DNA evidence.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Bogel-Burroughs and Jack Healy
item.media:credit: Pool photo by Trent Nelson
item.media:description: Sgt. David Hull, a Utah law enforcement officer who was one of the lead investigators into Charlie Kirk’s killing, during a hearing in Provo, Utah, on Tuesday.

item.title: Trump Administration Guts Efforts to Prevent Gun Violence, Suppressing Reports
item.description: As the administration works to expand gun rights, hospitals and community groups have lost funding, and reports on preventing firearm injuries have vanished from government websites.
item.dc:creator: Sheryl Gay Stolberg
item.category: Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives
Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang for The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Christopher S. Murphy of Connecticut has pushed to make the White House Office of Gun Violence Prevention permanent after President Trump shuttered the office, which originated under former President Joseph R. Biden Jr.

item.title: A Supreme Court Case on AR-15s Could Hinge on Their ‘Common Use’
item.description: Last week, the Supreme Court took up challenges to two assault weapons bans. Its decision could seriously limit the government’s ability to ban certain guns.
item.dc:creator: Mike McIntire
item.media:credit: Maansi Srivastava/The New York Times
item.media:description: Two new cases present the Supreme Court with its clearest opportunity yet to explain what kinds of guns are covered by earlier rulings.

item.title: Abandoned by Allies, Graham Platner Faces Pressure to End Maine Senate Campaign
item.description: After Graham Platner, the Democratic nominee in Maine, was accused of rape, much of the party and several key supporters turned against him.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Lerer and Katie Glueck
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Calls mounted for Graham Platner to end his Senate campaign.

item.title: After Courting Black Voters, Trump Downplays Black Unemployment
item.description: The president has glossed over persistently high unemployment among African Americans, a group he needed to secure a second term.
item.dc:creator: Erica L. Green
item.media:credit: Tom Brenner for The New York Times
item.media:description: President in the Oval Office on June 4, when he said that the opening of new factories was a reason for Black workers to have hope.
